caseMetadata.title: The Second Toast
caseMetadata.shortDescription: A classic contained whodunit about a fatal second toast at a private family dinner.
caseMetadata.notes: Draft package generated from sealed final implementation handoff; ready for package-level validation.

playerConfig.settingSummary: Vale House dining room after a private birthday dinner turns fatal.
playerConfig.playerRole: A private investigator retained by the family solicitor after the sudden death at dinner.
contentBoundaries: Poison details remain simple and unnamed during play.
No external lab or police-procedure branch.

setting.era: present day with classic whodunit atmosphere
setting.socialContext: A wealthy family gathers around an aging matriarch, a pending legal change, and old resentments.
setting.technologyLevel: Contemporary household and legal documents; no technical mechanism dominates play.
setting.toneNotes: Contained, intimate, emotionally legible, and clue-driven.
setting.sensoryStyle: Candlelight, rain at tall windows, polished wood, cordial glasses, and a family room gone quiet.

centralMystery.openingProblem: Eleanor Vale died moments after the second toast at a private family dinner.
centralMystery.stakes: The death freezes a family legal decision, exposes competing motives, and threatens to turn grief into accusation.
centralMystery.playerObjective: Determine whether Eleanor's death was natural, accidental, suicide, or murder, and if murder identify who made the second toast fatal.
centralMystery.successCondition: The player names the responsible party and supports the accusation with motive, method, opportunity, and physical proof.

solution.motive: Eleanor was about to remove Julian from estate control and expose forged foundation withdrawals that would bring disgrace, investigation, and ruin.
solution.motiveProportionality: The motive is concrete and severe: loss of inheritance control, exposure of financial misconduct, public disgrace, criminal investigation, and financial ruin.
solution.method: Julian poisoned Eleanor's amber cordial glass during the refill interval before the second toast, using a vial hidden in his silver cigar case.
solution.opportunity: He stood at the sideboard during the refill, handled Eleanor's amber glass, and returned it before the second toast.
solution.means: He had the vial concealed in his cigar case, access to the sideboard, and knowledge that Eleanor used the amber cordial glass.
solution.concealmentPlan: He denied touching Eleanor's specific glass, encouraged natural-death and family-conflict explanations, and relied on the private dinner confusion to obscure the refill.
solution.culpritMistake: He returned the amber glass to the wrong side of Eleanor's place setting, exposing that someone unfamiliar with her left-side placement habit had handled it.

proofRequiredForAccusation.deductionRequired: The player must establish that the fatal substance was in the second-toast cordial, that the first toast was safe, that Julian handled the glass during the critical interval, that the wrong-side placement contradicts his denial, that the codicil and withdrawals give him a concrete reason to act that night, and that the cigar-case stain provides physical proof from Julian's personal object.
proofRequiredForAccusation.insufficientAccusationResponse: That theory fits some facts, but it is not yet proven. The player still needs the full chain: timing, glass handling, placement contradiction, concrete motive, and physical proof from Julian's personal object.

finalRevealBeats: The death follows the second toast, not the meal.
The first toast was safe, narrowing the fatal interval.
Julian handled the amber glass during the refill interval while claiming he did not.
The wrong-side placement shows the glass was handled by someone who knew the glass but not Eleanor's placement habit.
The unsigned codicil and withdrawal note give Julian a concrete motive to act that night.
The cigar-case stain connects Julian's personal object to the dosing opportunity.
Beatrice, Mara, and Felix each have suspicion but lack the full combination of motive, method, opportunity, and proof.

postResolutionAnswers.canonicalEndgameExplanation: Julian Vale killed Eleanor before she could sign a codicil removing him from estate control and exposing forged withdrawals. He dosed her amber cordial glass during the refill before the second toast, using a vial hidden in his silver cigar case, then returned the glass to the wrong side of her place setting.
postResolutionAnswers.fallbackSolutionReveal: If gameplay stops early and the player asks out of game for the answer, reveal the authored solution from this package without improvising or adding new facts.
postResolutionAnswers.innocentSuspectClearance: Beatrice had anger but not glass access. Mara benefited but did not handle the glass and noticed the placement error. Felix knew the codicil but lacked physical opportunity.

characters.name: Julian Vale
characters.relationshipToMystery: He managed the dinner, the estate, and the refill interval at the sideboard.
characters.apparentMotive: Preserving the estate and avoiding family scandal.
characters.trueMotiveOrInnocentExplanation: He needed to prevent a legal change and conceal forged foundation withdrawals.
characters.alibiClaim: He says he only steadied the decanter and never touched Eleanor's specific glass.
characters.alibiTruth: He handled Eleanor's amber glass and dosed it during the refill.
knowledge: Eleanor used the short amber cordial glass.
His cigar case concealed the vial.
secrets: He used the silver cigar case to conceal the vial.
liesOrOmissions: He frames Eleanor as frail and emotional.

playerQuestionExamples: What did you do during the refill?
Did you touch Eleanor's glass?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: I stood to steady the decanter and keep the evening from becoming a scene.
interviewTopics.lieOrOmission: He denies touching Eleanor's specific glass.
interviewTopics.evasiveAnswer: Everyone saw me at the sideboard. That hardly makes me a murderer.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: He will not volunteer the vial, cigar case, or forged withdrawals.
interviewTopics.emotionalState: Polished and protective, with tension beneath.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: Julian repeats that he only steadied the decanter.

playerQuestionExamples: Did you know what Eleanor planned to sign?
Were you losing control of the estate?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: I knew Felix brought papers. Aunt Eleanor changed her mind often.
interviewTopics.lieOrOmission: He claims not to know the document threatened his control.
interviewTopics.evasiveAnswer: This is not the time to turn grief into paperwork.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: He knows the codicil and withdrawals threatened him but hides both.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: He insists the document was not his concern.
interviewTopics.validationNotes: Motive pressure should be driven by document evidence.

playerQuestionExamples: Why is your cigar case on the sideboard?
May I inspect the cigar case?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: It is a family keepsake. I put it down after dinner.
interviewTopics.lieOrOmission: He hides that it held the vial.
interviewTopics.evasiveAnswer: Surely you are not accusing a cigar case.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: He knows the stain and empty sleeve are dangerous to him.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: He says the case is irrelevant.
interviewTopics.validationNotes: Physical proof path after opportunity is established.

characters.interrogationGuidance: Do not have Julian confess unless confronted with timing, glass handling, codicil motive, and cigar-case evidence.

characters.name: Mara Vale
characters.relationshipToMystery: She was seated beside Eleanor and was named in the planned trustee change.
characters.apparentMotive: She would gain foundation authority if the codicil were signed.
characters.trueMotiveOrInnocentExplanation: She wanted the foundation cleaned up but did not know the full financial misconduct until that night and did not handle the glass.
characters.alibiClaim: She stayed seated beside Eleanor during the refill.
characters.alibiTruth: She stayed in place and noticed the glass placement mistake.
knowledge: Eleanor planned to change foundation trustees.
Old family guilt around foundation money.
secrets: She argued with Eleanor years earlier over family money.
liesOrOmissions: She is reluctant to talk about old family conflict.

playerQuestionExamples: Did Eleanor always use that glass?
Was the glass where it normally sat?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: Eleanor used the amber glass because it was easier on her hands, and she usually kept it to her left.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: Mara knows the habit and placement, not who dosed the glass.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: Mara repeats that the glass being on the right was wrong.
interviewTopics.validationNotes: Key observation that also helps clear Mara through lack of handling.

playerQuestionExamples: Were you going to benefit from the document?
Did Eleanor plan to name you trustee?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: She wanted me to help clean up the foundation. I did not know how bad it was until tonight.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: Mara knows the planned appointment, not every detail of Julian's withdrawals until shown evidence.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: Mara says the change was about repair, not a prize.

playerQuestionExamples: Did you argue with Eleanor?
Were you estranged from her?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: We argued years ago about money and the foundation. I regretted how long I stayed away.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: Old guilt does not give her method or opportunity.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: The old argument still hurts her, but it does not explain the glass.

characters.interrogationGuidance: Mara should provide the key glass observation without solving the case for the player.

characters.name: Beatrice Hale
characters.relationshipToMystery: She argued with Eleanor over inheritance and family legacy during dinner.
characters.apparentMotive: Visible anger over Eleanor giving assets to the foundation.
characters.trueMotiveOrInnocentExplanation: She wanted reconciliation and money but lacked the key glass access and habit knowledge.
characters.alibiClaim: She moved to the window after the first toast.
characters.alibiTruth: She was away from the sideboard during the refill.
secrets: She tore up a reconciliation letter.
liesOrOmissions: She minimizes the anger of the dinner argument.

playerQuestionExamples: What were you arguing about?
Did you resent the foundation?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: I said ugly things. I thought she was giving the family away piece by piece.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: Beatrice knows her anger but not the method.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: She admits the argument but not murder.

playerQuestionExamples: Where were you during the refill?
Did you go near the sideboard?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: I went to the window because I was crying and did not want Eleanor to see.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: She knows her own movement and little about the sideboard.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: She repeats that she was by the window.

playerQuestionExamples: What is the torn letter?
Were you trying to reconcile?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: It was a stupid attempt to say I was sorry. I tore it up before dinner.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: The letter is emotional context, not method.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: She says the letter was apology, not a plan.
interviewTopics.validationNotes: Supports red herring without adding a new branch.

characters.interrogationGuidance: Beatrice should look emotionally suspicious but not have method or access.

characters.name: Felix Morrow
characters.relationshipToMystery: He brought the unsigned codicil Eleanor intended to sign.
characters.apparentMotive: He knew the document contents and could benefit from legal confusion.
characters.trueMotiveOrInnocentExplanation: He is professionally nervous but had no access to the cordial glasses during the refill.
characters.alibiClaim: He was retrieving the document folder from his briefcase after the second toast began.
characters.alibiTruth: He had the folder but no glass access during the refill.
knowledge: The legal effect of Eleanor's planned change.
liesOrOmissions: He initially avoids stating the codicil's full effect in front of the family.

playerQuestionExamples: What was Eleanor going to sign?
Who would the document affect?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: The codicil would remove Julian from estate management and name Mara as trustee.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: Felix knows the document contents but not the murderer's action.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: Felix repeats the legal effect of the unsigned codicil.

playerQuestionExamples: Was the document signed?
When were you going to present it?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: It was not signed. I was about to bring it out after the second toast.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: Felix can confirm unsigned status and timing.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: The document remained unsigned when Eleanor died.

playerQuestionExamples: Where were you during the refill?
Did you touch the glasses?
interviewTopics.truthfulAnswer: I was at my briefcase, away from the cordial glasses.
interviewTopics.knowledgeBoundary: Felix can account for his own movement.
interviewTopics.repeatAnswer: He repeats that he was at the briefcase, not the sideboard.

characters.interrogationGuidance: Felix should disclose legal motive when pressed, but not become a solution narrator.

locations.name: Vale House dining room
locations.description: A formal dining room after a private dinner. Eleanor's chair sits near one end of the table. A sideboard holds a cordial decanter, several glasses, Julian's silver cigar case, and the solicitor's document folder.
exitLeads: Question suspects about the refill and toasts.

timeline.summary: Felix arrives with the unsigned codicil.
timeline.cause: Eleanor has asked him to bring legal papers for signing.
timeline.effect: The document becomes available in the dining room but remains unsigned.
timeline.visibilityToPlayer: Discoverable through Felix and the folder.

timeline.summary: Eleanor privately tells Julian his management role will end.
timeline.cause: She has decided to change control of the foundation.
timeline.effect: Julian realizes he may be exposed that night.
timeline.visibilityToPlayer: Hidden until document evidence is found.

timeline.summary: Beatrice argues that Eleanor is giving the family away to charity.
timeline.cause: Long-running family resentment and inheritance pressure.

timeline.summary: The first toast occurs and Eleanor drinks safely from the amber cordial glass.
timeline.effect: The fatal dosing must occur later.

timeline.summary: Julian stands at the sideboard to steady the decanter and refill Eleanor's amber glass.
timeline.cause: A brief refill before the second toast.
timeline.effect: Julian gains access to the glass.

timeline.summary: Julian doses the amber glass using a vial hidden in his silver cigar case.
timeline.cause: He needs Eleanor dead before signing.
timeline.effect: The second toast becomes fatal and the cigar case retains physical trace.
timeline.visibilityToPlayer: Hidden until physical evidence and placement are discovered.

timeline.summary: The second toast occurs; Eleanor drinks, falters, and collapses.
timeline.visibilityToPlayer: Opening fact, with cause discoverable through inspection.

timeline.summary: The player arrives after Felix calls for outside help before police take over.
timeline.cause: The family solicitor requests immediate private investigation.
timeline.effect: The player can inspect the room and question the four suspects.

clues.title: Poisoned amber cordial glass
clues.playerFacingDescription: The amber glass smells sharper than the untouched cordial on the sideboard.
clues.trueMeaning: Death followed the second-toast cordial, not dinner food.
clues.discoveryConditions: Inspect Eleanor's amber glass or ask what she drank.
clues.resolvedInFinalReveal: It establishes the fatal delivery point.
clues.textFallback: The amber glass has a sharp scent that differs from the untouched cordial.

clues.title: First toast was safe
clues.playerFacingDescription: Eleanor drank from the amber glass during the first toast and seemed well afterward.
clues.trueMeaning: The glass became dangerous between the first and second toast.
clues.discoveryConditions: Question Felix or Mara about the dinner sequence.
clues.textFallback: The first toast did not harm Eleanor.

clues.title: Julian handled Eleanor's glass
clues.playerFacingDescription: Julian stood at the sideboard during the refill and handled the amber glass despite later denial.
clues.trueMeaning: He had opportunity during the critical interval.
clues.discoveryConditions: Question Julian, Mara, or Felix about the refill.
clues.resolvedInFinalReveal: It connects Julian to the opportunity.
clues.textFallback: Julian was the person at the sideboard handling the refill.

clues.title: Amber glass returned to wrong side
clues.playerFacingDescription: Eleanor normally kept the amber glass on her left, but after the refill it was on her right.
clues.trueMeaning: Someone handled and replaced the glass without knowing her left-side placement habit.
clues.discoveryConditions: Ask Mara about Eleanor's habits or inspect the table setting.
clues.resolvedInFinalReveal: It contradicts Julian's denial and exposes his mistake.
clues.textFallback: The glass is on the wrong side for Eleanor's habit.

clues.title: Codicil would remove Julian from control
clues.playerFacingDescription: The unsigned codicil would remove Julian as estate manager and name Mara trustee.
clues.trueMeaning: Julian had a concrete reason to stop Eleanor before signing.
clues.discoveryConditions: Inspect the solicitor folder or question Felix about the codicil.
clues.resolvedInFinalReveal: It establishes why Julian had to act that night.
clues.textFallback: The document would remove Julian from estate management.

clues.title: Foundation withdrawals under Julian's control
clues.playerFacingDescription: Eleanor's note asks Felix about Julian's withdrawals before signing.
clues.trueMeaning: Julian's motive is exposure of misconduct, not vague inheritance pressure.
clues.discoveryConditions: Inspect Eleanor's note in the solicitor folder or question Felix after finding the codicil.
clues.sourceOrProvenance: Eleanor's note in the solicitor folder.
clues.textFallback: Eleanor planned to ask Felix about Julian's withdrawals before signing.

clues.title: Cordial stain inside silver cigar case
clues.playerFacingDescription: Inside the silver cigar case is a sticky amber stain and one narrow empty sleeve.
clues.trueMeaning: The case concealed or contacted the dosing vial.
clues.discoveryConditions: Closely inspect Julian's cigar case, open the cigar case, search the sideboard, look for something Julian left near the glasses, or inspect Julian's personal items on the sideboard after establishing he stood at the sideboard.
clues.sourceOrProvenance: Julian's silver cigar case on the sideboard.
clues.resolvedInFinalReveal: It supplies the physical proof connecting Julian's object to the dosing opportunity.
clues.textFallback: The cigar case has a faint amber stain and an empty narrow sleeve.

clues.title: Mara knew the glass habit but never handled the glass
clues.playerFacingDescription: Mara knew Eleanor's left-side glass habit and stayed seated during the refill.
clues.trueMeaning: Mara supplies the observation but lacks the handling opportunity.
clues.discoveryConditions: Question Mara about the glass and her position.
clues.resolvedInFinalReveal: It clears Mara while preserving her useful observation.
clues.textFallback: Mara knew the habit but did not handle the glass.

clues.title: Beatrice's inheritance anger
clues.playerFacingDescription: Beatrice argued that Eleanor was giving the family away to charity.
clues.trueMeaning: Beatrice had emotional pressure but not the required method or opportunity.
clues.discoveryConditions: Question Beatrice about the dinner argument.
clues.resolvedInFinalReveal: It explains the inheritance red herring.
clues.textFallback: Beatrice had anger but not proof of method.

clues.title: Beatrice moved to the window
clues.playerFacingDescription: Beatrice moved away from the table and sideboard during the refill.
clues.trueMeaning: She did not have the key glass opportunity.
clues.textFallback: Beatrice was at the window during the refill.

clues.title: Unsigned document
clues.playerFacingDescription: The codicil was not signed before Eleanor died.
clues.trueMeaning: The timing of the death preserves the old arrangement long enough to matter.
clues.discoveryConditions: Question Felix about the document timing.
clues.resolvedInFinalReveal: It explains why the death had to happen before signing.

clues.title: Place cards and sight lines
clues.playerFacingDescription: The place cards confirm who sat near Eleanor, the window, sideboard, and briefcase.
clues.trueMeaning: The seating helps test opportunity without adding new witnesses.
clues.textFallback: The seating shows who could plausibly reach the sideboard and glass.

redHerrings.title: Beatrice's inheritance anger
redHerrings.appearsToSuggest: Beatrice killed Eleanor because she resented the family wealth going to the foundation.
redHerrings.innocentExplanation: Her anger is real, but she did not know the amber-glass habit, moved away from the sideboard, and did not handle the glass.
redHerrings.discoveryConditions: Question Beatrice about the argument and movement.
redHerrings.clearanceConditions: Discover Beatrice's window movement and lack of glass access.
redHerrings.fairnessNotes: Visible emotional pressure remains fair because opportunity clears her.

redHerrings.title: Mara as beneficiary
redHerrings.appearsToSuggest: Mara killed Eleanor because the codicil would give her foundation authority.
redHerrings.innocentExplanation: Mara did not handle the glass, and her observation about the wrong-side placement exposes the method rather than concealing it.
redHerrings.discoveryConditions: Question Mara and inspect or read the codicil.
redHerrings.clearanceConditions: Discover her glass-habit observation and lack of handling opportunity.
redHerrings.fairnessNotes: Benefit alone is not enough without method or opportunity.

redHerrings.title: Felix's legal control
redHerrings.appearsToSuggest: Felix manipulated the signing or death to control the legal confusion.
redHerrings.innocentExplanation: Felix knew the codicil contents but lacked access to the cordial glasses during the refill, and his testimony points away from himself.
redHerrings.discoveryConditions: Question Felix about codicil and briefcase timing.
redHerrings.clearanceConditions: Establish he was at the briefcase and did not handle the glasses.
redHerrings.fairnessNotes: Knowledge is suspicious but not equivalent to physical opportunity.

evidence.title: Amber cordial glass
evidence.description: The small amber cordial glass at Eleanor's place, with a sharper scent than untouched cordial.
chainOfCustody: Used by Eleanor during the first and second toasts.
accessHistory: On the table near Eleanor's place.
Available for player inspection at start.

evidence.title: Unsigned codicil folder
evidence.description: Felix's folder containing the unsigned codicil.
chainOfCustody: Brought by Felix for Eleanor to sign.
Left on or near the sideboard after the collapse.
evidence.discoveryConditions: Read the solicitor folder or question Felix.

evidence.title: Eleanor's withdrawal note
evidence.description: A note in the solicitor folder about Julian's withdrawals.
chainOfCustody: Written by Eleanor before the dinner.
Placed with the papers Felix brought.
evidence.discoveryConditions: Read Eleanor's note in the solicitor folder.

evidence.title: Silver cigar case
evidence.description: Julian's silver cigar case, containing a faint amber stain and an empty narrow sleeve.
chainOfCustody: Set down on the sideboard during dinner.
accessHistory: Inspectable after player attention turns to the refill or sideboard.
evidence.discoveryConditions: Closely inspect the cigar case after establishing Julian's sideboard access.

evidence.title: Beatrice's torn letter
evidence.description: A torn reconciliation letter in Beatrice's possession.
chainOfCustody: Written and torn by Beatrice before dinner.
accessHistory: Only relevant if Beatrice discusses it or the player asks about her visible distress.
evidence.discoveryConditions: Question Beatrice about the torn letter.

evidence.title: Dining table place cards
evidence.description: Place cards showing the dinner seating and sight lines.
evidence.discoveryConditions: Inspect the place cards and table setting.

scenes.openingNarration: Eleanor Vale's birthday dinner ended before the second toast could settle. The dining room is still warm with candlelight and rain taps against the tall windows. Eleanor's chair sits slightly pushed back from the table. Beside her plate is a small amber cordial glass, untouched now except for the last sip she managed before whispering, "No. Not this."
likelyPlayerQuestions: Where was everyone during the refill?
Can I ask about poison testing?

safeAnswers.natural_death: The question is fair. The dining-room evidence can test whether the second toast, rather than the meal or natural collapse, explains the death.
safeAnswers.who_benefited: Several people had pressure: Julian could lose control, Mara could gain authority, Beatrice had family resentment, and Felix knew the document contents.
safeAnswers.lab_tests: That is a reasonable question. Police and medical testing will follow, but the immediate playable proof can proceed from in-room facts: timing, glass access, motive, and physical evidence at the table and sideboard.
safeAnswers.more_witnesses: No additional interviewable witnesses are needed; the case is contained in the dining room.

hiddenFactsNotYetRevealed: The culprit's handling of the glass.
exitLeads: Inspect the silver cigar case after establishing the refill.

scenes.entryConditions: Authored leads are exhausted or the player asks for deduction help.
scenes.openingNarration: The remaining task is deduction: reconcile the two toasts, the glass placement, the document, the sideboard access, and the physical object on the sideboard.

safeAnswers.balanced_recap: A neutral recap should not over-foreground any single suspect. Include Beatrice's visible anger and opportunity problems, Mara's codicil benefit and lack of glass handling plus her placement observation, Felix's document knowledge and lack of glass access during the refill, and Julian's strongest combination only if motive, refill access, placement contradiction, and cigar-case physical evidence are all established.
safeAnswers.early_accusation: A correct theory may be plausible, but do not confirm the solution or narrate the final reconstruction until the full proof chain is discovered: timing, glass handling, placement contradiction, concrete motive, and physical proof from Julian's personal object.

hiddenFactsNotYetRevealed: Full final resolution until accusation or spoiler request.
exitLeads: Make a final accusation with suspect, motive, method, opportunity, and proof.

discoveryRules.description: Player observes the dining room at the start of play.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: The room holds the aftermath of a private dinner; no other location is needed to begin.
discoveryRules.repeatText: The dining room still holds the available leads.
discoveryRules.validationNotes: Opening observation should not reveal the cause of death.

discoveryRules.description: Player inspects Eleanor's amber cordial glass.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: The amber glass smells sharper than the untouched cordial on the sideboard.
discoveryRules.failureText: Do not taste evidence; the glass should be isolated. Odor, residue, placement, and witness timing are safe to inspect.
discoveryRules.repeatText: The amber glass remains the central physical evidence.
discoveryRules.validationNotes: No named poison or lab branch.

discoveryRules.description: Player asks Felix or Mara about the two toasts.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: Eleanor drank from the same amber glass during the first toast and seemed well afterward.
discoveryRules.repeatText: The first toast was safe; the danger appeared before the second.

discoveryRules.description: Player questions Julian about the sideboard and refill.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: Julian admits he stood at the sideboard during the refill but denies touching Eleanor's specific glass.
discoveryRules.repeatText: Julian continues to deny handling the specific glass.

discoveryRules.description: Player asks Mara about Eleanor's glass habits.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: Eleanor always kept the amber glass on her left; after the refill it was on her right.
discoveryRules.repeatText: Mara repeats that the glass placement was wrong.
discoveryRules.validationNotes: Exposes placement error and helps clear Mara.

discoveryRules.discoveryText: The unsigned codicil would remove Julian and name Mara trustee.
discoveryRules.repeatText: The codicil remains unsigned and would have changed control.

discoveryRules.description: Player reads Eleanor's note in the solicitor folder.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: Eleanor had written: "Ask Felix about Julian's withdrawals before I sign. No more delays."
discoveryRules.failureText: The motive is not concrete until the folder and note are examined.
discoveryRules.repeatText: The note still points to Julian's withdrawals.

discoveryRules.description: Player closely inspects Julian's silver cigar case or takes a reasonable equivalent action around Julian's personal item on the sideboard.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: Inside the case is a sticky amber stain matching the cordial scent; one narrow sleeve is empty.
discoveryRules.failureText: A casual look at the sideboard does not prove anything yet. Once Julian's sideboard access is established, inspecting or opening his cigar case is a fair next step.
discoveryRules.repeatText: The cigar case still links a personal object to the dosing opportunity.

discoveryRules.description: Player questions Beatrice about argument and movement.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: Beatrice was angry, but she moved to the window during the refill.
discoveryRules.repeatText: Beatrice's anger remains real, but her movement pulls her away from the sideboard.

discoveryRules.description: Player questions Felix about the codicil.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: Felix confirms the codicil would remove Julian from management and was unsigned when Eleanor died.
discoveryRules.repeatText: Felix repeats the codicil's effect and unsigned status.
discoveryRules.validationNotes: Felix supplies motive information but not the solution.

discoveryRules.description: Player inspects table placement and place cards.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: The place cards confirm who sat near Eleanor, the window, sideboard, and briefcase.
discoveryRules.repeatText: The seating still helps test opportunity.

discoveryRules.description: Player makes a final accusation against Julian.
discoveryRules.discoveryText: The accusation is supported by the complete proof chain: timing, glass handling, placement contradiction, concrete motive, and physical proof from Julian's personal object.
discoveryRules.failureText: That theory fits some facts, but it is not yet proven. You still need the full chain: timing, glass handling, placement contradiction, concrete motive, and physical proof from Julian's personal object.
discoveryRules.repeatText: The accusation turns on the same proof chain.
discoveryRules.validationNotes: Do not confirm the solution or reveal the full canonical reconstruction unless all prerequisite clues, including clue-cigar-case-stain, have been discovered.

knownFacts: Eleanor Vale died shortly after the second toast at a private family dinner.
The death occurred in the Vale House dining room.
A small amber cordial glass sat beside Eleanor's place.
Four people were present: Julian, Mara, Beatrice, and Felix.
openQuestions: Did Eleanor die naturally, accidentally, or by murder?
Why did the second toast become fatal if the first toast was safe?
Who handled Eleanor's amber cordial glass?
What document did Felix bring for Eleanor to sign?
Which suspect had motive, method, and opportunity?
activeLeads: Question the suspects about the two toasts.
Inspect the sideboard and cordial decanter.
Ask Felix about the unsigned document.

assetManifest.title: Vale House dining room
assetManifest.discoveryConditions: Optional on player request; image mode is off by default.
assetManifest.textFallback: A candlelit formal dining room with Eleanor's chair, an amber cordial glass, a sideboard, a decanter, place cards, and a solicitor's folder.
assetManifest.imagePromptGuidance: Show only authored room objects. Do not depict the culprit action, hidden poison, extra people, extra doors, or visual clues not already available in text.

assetManifest.title: Amber cordial glass
assetManifest.discoveryConditions: Optional after the glass has been inspected in text.
assetManifest.textFallback: A small amber cordial glass sits at Eleanor's place, slightly misplaced from where Mara says Eleanor normally kept it.
assetManifest.imagePromptGuidance: Support text discoveries only. Do not add labels, fingerprints, visible poison, or extra hidden markings.

assetManifest.title: Unsigned codicil folder
assetManifest.discoveryConditions: Optional after the solicitor folder has been read in text.
assetManifest.textFallback: The unsigned codicil would remove Julian as estate manager and name Mara as trustee.
assetManifest.imagePromptGuidance: Show only document fragments already revealed in text. Do not expose undiscovered solution facts visually.

assetManifest.title: Silver cigar case
assetManifest.discoveryConditions: Optional after the cigar case has been closely inspected in text.
assetManifest.textFallback: A silver cigar case with a faint amber stain inside and one narrow empty sleeve.
assetManifest.imagePromptGuidance: Support text discovery only. Do not add labels, names, or visual details that identify the culprit before textual discovery.

knownRiskAreas: Do not open a lab, autopsy, or toxicology branch.
Keep recaps balanced unless the player asks for a hint or theory check.
Treat early correct guesses as plausible but unproven without the required clue chain.
Clarify ambiguous voice input instead of guessing.

canonicalAssetInventory.notes: All investigative content is contained in the dining room. Staff, police, and laboratory work are out of scope.

runtimeBudgets.notes: Contained Quick Mystery: one primary location, four suspects, no external forensic branch.

visualDefinitions.canonicalDescription: A candlelit formal dining room with Eleanor's chair, an amber cordial glass, a sideboard, a decanter, place cards, and a solicitor's folder.
visualDefinitions.fixedGeometryNotes: Do not add additional rooms, hidden doors, servants, or visible poison.
hiddenElementRules: Do not reveal solution facts visually.
visualDefinitions.continuityAnchor: Eleanor's chair at table, sideboard on wall, amber glass at her place.
visualDefinitions.textFallback: A candlelit formal dining room with Eleanor's chair, an amber cordial glass, a sideboard, a decanter, place cards, and a solicitor's folder.
visualDefinitions.validationNotes: Scene image is optional and supportive only.

visualDefinitions.canonicalDescription: A small amber cordial glass at Eleanor's place.
visualDefinitions.fixedGeometryNotes: Do not show visible poison, labels, fingerprints, or new marks.
hiddenElementRules: Only support details already discovered in text.
visualDefinitions.continuityAnchor: Amber glass near Eleanor's place setting.
visualDefinitions.textFallback: A small amber cordial glass sits at Eleanor's place, slightly misplaced from where Mara says Eleanor normally kept it.

visualDefinitions.canonicalDescription: A solicitor's folder with the already-read codicil text summarized in conversation.
visualDefinitions.fixedGeometryNotes: Do not add undiscovered document text.
hiddenElementRules: Show only facts already revealed in text.
visualDefinitions.textFallback: The unsigned codicil would remove Julian as estate manager and name Mara as trustee.

visualDefinitions.canonicalDescription: A silver cigar case with a faint amber stain and an empty narrow sleeve after textual discovery.
visualDefinitions.fixedGeometryNotes: Do not add labels, names, or extra mechanisms.
hiddenElementRules: Only show after the cigar case has been inspected in text.
visualDefinitions.continuityAnchor: Silver cigar case from the sideboard.
visualDefinitions.textFallback: A silver cigar case with a faint amber stain inside and one narrow empty sleeve.
visualDefinitions.validationNotes: High spoiler asset; optional only after discovery.

imageGalleryPolicy.notes: Assign displayed images a runtime number and reuse prior images on recall instead of regenerating.

imageReusePolicy.regenerationAllowedWhen: Only when no prior image exists or the player asks for an authored new view.
imageReusePolicy.notes: Text is canonical. If image generation cannot preserve fidelity, provide text fallback.
